model.label: DeepSeek V3.2
model.description: DeepSeek • General-purpose V-series model optimized for reasoning, coding, and efficient inference.

pick.rationale: Vanderbilt's elite top-10 offense, combined with their higher overall efficiency ratings, gives them an edge over Nebraska's stout defense. While Nebraska's defense is formidable, Vanderbilt's balanced attack and the prediction from analyst Jay Bilas that their guards will be the difference support a Commodores victory. In a tight contest, Vanderbilt's offensive firepower should prevail.

reasoningStep.title: Analysis of Vanderbilt vs. Nebraska Round of 32 Matchup
reasoningStep.summary: Evaluated team ratings, matchup strengths, and expert predictions to determine Vanderbilt as the pick.
evidence: Vanderbilt has superior offensive efficiency (Torvik adjoe rank 6, KenPom adj_o rank 6) and overall ratings (Torvik rank 10, KenPom rank 12) compared to Nebraska's elite defense (Torvik adjde rank 7) but weaker offense (Torvik adjoe rank 62). ESPN analyst Jay Bilas predicts Vanderbilt to win, citing their guards as the difference-maker. No significant injury concerns were found for either team from available searches.

toolCalls.summary: Returned combined Torvik/KenPom ratings for 2 teams.

(2:42) Jay Bilas Mar 16, 2026, 09:00 AM ET Close College basketball analyst for ESPN and ESPN Insider Played and coached at Duke Practicing attorney Multiple Authors Email Print Open Extended Reactions You already know the drill. After my fellow analysts and I have the privilege of calling the SEC championship game in Nashville, Tennessee, we move swiftly to process the men's NCAA tournament field as it is revealed. Our bosses at ESPN ask us each to fill out a bracket -- while we are on the air -- so we can reveal our picks before the end of the show. They give us less than five minutes. Hardly ideal, but perhaps our first instincts are better than considered thought. I decided that I would give you more than five minutes so you can dazzle your friends and win your office pool -- and perhaps our Tournament Challenge. Last season, my bracket was chalky : I had all four No. 1 seeds reaching the Final Four, Houston and Florida advancing to the championship game, and the Gators cutting down the nets. The backlash was swift and unrelenting. I was criticized for not picking an underdog to reach San Antonio. B

modelTrace.content:
You are an analyst predicting a single March Madness game inside a bracket run.
You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.
Final JSON keys: pick, reasoningStep.
pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.
The rationale must be a short paragraph of 2 to 4 sentences explaining why the winner was chosen.
reasoningStep must contain id, title, summary, evidence.

Nebraska is also strong (Torvik #19, KenPom #14) with an elite defense (#7 in Torvik) and a solid offense (#62 in Torvik, #55 in KenPom). This looks like a classic offense vs. defense matchup. Vanderbilt scores a lot but allows some points; Nebraska defends exceptionally but scores less efficiently.

(2:42) Jay Bilas Mar 16, 2026, 09:00 AM ET Close College basketball analyst for ESPN and ESPN Insider Played and coached at Duke Practicing attorney Multiple Authors Email Print Open Extended Reactions You already know the drill. After my fellow analysts and I have the privilege of calling the SEC championship game in Nashville, Tennessee, we move swiftly to process the men's NCAA tournament field as it is revealed. Our bosses at ESPN ask us each to fill out a bracket -- while we are on the air -- so we can reveal our picks before the end of the show. They give us less than five minutes. Hardly ideal, but perhaps our first instincts are better than considered thought. I decided that I would give you more than five minutes so you can dazzle your friends and win your office pool -- and perhaps our Tournament Challenge. Last season, my bracket was chalky : I had all four No. 1 seeds reaching the Final Four, Houston and Florida advancing to the championship game, and the Gators cutting down the nets. The backlash was swift and unrelenting. I was criticized for not picking an underdog to reach San Antonio. B
